lesson.menu_desc: Phase 2 -- the passive voice in everyday processes and instructions -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 14 -- Make It Happen -- The Passive Voice
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Mark Kazuyoshi Seki Omagari | Aula 14 -- Make It Happen

listenings.text: Hi Mark. Last class we looked back at the past and imagined different outcomes with should have and could have. Today we learn how to describe a process or a set of instructions, and for that English loves the passive voice. Here is the key idea. In the active voice, we say who does the action: the team installs the app. In the passive voice, we put the result first and the doer becomes less important: the app is installed. The form is always a form of be plus the past participle. In the present, we say the order is processed and the files are submitted. In the past, we say the system was monitored and the bugs were handled. If the doer matters, we add it with by, like the report was approved by the manager. But in a process, the doer is often obvious or general, so we just leave it out. Listen for is processed, are assembled, was triggered, and were disposed of. The passive is perfect when the action matters more than the person doing it. So think about something you use every day. How is it made? How is it delivered? See you in class, Mark.

Let me explain how my favorite app is updated, because I think the process is really clever. First, the new code is written by the developers and then it is submitted for review. Once it is approved, the rollout is started slowly. The update is not pushed to everyone at the same time. Instead, a small group is monitored first, and if a glitch is triggered, the release is paused immediately. The bottleneck is usually the testing stage, because every feature must be inspected before it is shipped. When the old files are no longer needed, they are disposed of safely, so storage is not wasted. I find it fascinating that the whole thing is streamlined to feel instant, even though so many steps are handled in the background. Nothing is left to chance: the process is assembled step by step, and a shortcut is never taken when safety is involved.

extra_audio.text: First, you decide what the result of the action is, because in the passive the result comes first. Then, you choose the correct form of the verb be: is or are for the present, was or were for the past. After that, you add the past participle of the main verb, like processed, assembled or handled. Next, if the doer is important, you add it at the end with the word by. Finally, if the doer is obvious or general, you simply leave it out, because in a process the action matters more than the person.
